metadata.name: py5canvas
metadata.summary: Library to create drawings and animations with an interface similar to p5js and Processing.

modules.name: py5canvas.sketch_params_pyimgui
documentation:
```
             eeeee
eeeee e    e 8     eeee eeeee eeeee ee   e eeeee eeeee
8   8 8    8 8eeee 8  8 8   8 8   8 88   8 8   8 8   "
8eee8 8eeee8     8 8e   8eee8 8e  8 88  e8 8eee8 8eeee
88      88       8 88   88  8 88  8  8  8  88  8    88
88      88   eeee8 88e8 88  8 88  8  8ee8  88  8 8ee88

```
A simple p5-inspired interface to the Canvas object
© Daniel Berio (@colormotor) 2023 - ...

NOTE: this is highly unoptimized, since it uses pycairo for rendering and copies all the screen at each frame
It will probably be significantly slow when using a large canvas size

documentation:
Makes sure options are available for all parameters
and creates a key entry for each paramter or group

functions.name: py5canvas.sketch_params_pyimgui.fetch_params
documentation: Fetches the parameters from the gui_params dictionary

documentation: Special json encoder for numpy types

classes.name: py5canvas.sketch_params_pyimgui.SketchParams

methods.name: py5canvas.sketch_params_pyimgui.SketchParams.__init__
documentation:
params: a (possibly nested) dictionary of parameters,
entries with a tuple or list consist of (value, options) pairs
nested dictionaries are treated as groups,
and will be displayed as such in the GUI

Handle the parameters of a sketch.
Stores two dictionaries, one for easy access to sketch parameters,
through EasyDict (if available) and one for the GUI.
Both can be nested dictionaries.

methods.name: py5canvas.run_sketch.Sketch.mouse_x
documentation: The horizontal coordinate of the mouse position

methods.name: py5canvas.run_sketch.Sketch.mouse_y
documentation: The vertical coordinate of the mouse position

methods.name: py5canvas.run_sketch.Sketch.frame_count
documentation: The number of frames since the script has loaded

methods.name: py5canvas.run_sketch.Sketch.open_file_dialog
documentation:
Opens a dialog to select a file to be opened,
the first argument is the extension or the file to be opened,
e.g. ~'png'~ or a list of extensions, e.g. ~['png', 'jpg']

The function returns the path of the file if it is selected or an empty string othewise.

methods.name: py5canvas.run_sketch.Sketch.save_file_dialog
documentation:
Opens a dialog to select a file to be saved,
the first argument is the extension or the file to be saved,
e.g. ~'png'~ or a list of extensions, e.g. ~['png', 'jpg']

The function returns the path of the file if it is selected or an empty string othewise.

methods.name: py5canvas.run_sketch.Sketch.open_folder_dialog
documentation:
Opens a dialog to select a folder/directory to be opened,

The function returns the path of the directory if it is selected or an empty string othewise.

methods.name: py5canvas.run_sketch.Sketch.save_canvas
documentation: Tells the sketch to dump the next frame to an SVG file

methods.name: py5canvas.run_sketch.Sketch.toggle_fullscreen
documentation: Toggle between fullscreen and windowed mode

methods.name: py5canvas.run_sketch.Sketch.fullscreen
documentation: Sets fullscreen or windowed mode depending on the first argument (~True~ or ~False~)

methods.name: py5canvas.run_sketch.Sketch.no_loop
documentation: Stops the drawing loop keeping the last frame fixed on the canvas

methods.name: py5canvas.run_sketch.Sketch.grab_image_sequence
documentation:
Saves a sequence of image files to a directory, one for each frame.
By default this will reload the current script.

Arguments:
- ~path~ (string), the directory where to save the images
- ~num_frames~ (int), the number of frames to save

methods.name: py5canvas.run_sketch.Sketch.grab_movie
documentation:
Saves a mp4 movie from a number of frames to a specified path.
By default this will reload the current script.

Arguments:
- ~path~ (string), the directory where to save the video
- ~num_frames~ (int), the number of frames to save

methods.name: py5canvas.run_sketch.Sketch.title
documentation: Sets the title of the sketch window

methods.name: py5canvas.run_sketch.Sketch.frame_rate
documentation: Set the framerate of the sketch in frames-per-second

In Py5Canvas a sketch is a Python script with a custom defined ~setup~ and ~draw~ functions,
that allow to create interactive apps in a way similar to P5js or Processing. For the system to work
you must defiine a sketch similar to what follows:

#+begin_src python
from py5canvas import *

def setup():
    create_canvas(400, 400)

def draw():
    background(0)

run()
#+end_src

Canvas drawing code can go in either ~draw~ or ~setup~. To run a sketch, simply run the script from
a terminal or from your editor of choice. For the system to work, you must import Py5Canvas at the beginning
and call ~run~ at the end.

modules.name: py5canvas.turtle
documentation:
© Daniel Berio (@colormotor) 2021 - ...
turtle - 2d turtle implementation (crudely adapted from Fogelman's axi module)

functions.name: py5canvas.globals.range_between
documentation:
Returns a list of numbers that goes from a and b in a specified number of steps.

E.g. ~range_betwee(0, 1, 5)~ will give the list ~[0.0, 0.25, 0.5, 0.75, 1.0]~

Similar to ~np.linspace~

functions.name: py5canvas.globals.linspace
documentation:
Returns a list of numbers that goes from a and b in a specified number of steps.

E.g. ~linspace(0, 1, 5)~ will give the list ~[0.0, 0.25, 0.5, 0.75, 1.0]~

Similar to ~np.linspace~

functions.name: py5canvas.globals.arange
documentation:
Returns a list of numbers that goes from a and b with equal steps

E.g. ~arange(0, 1, 0.25)~ will give the list ~[0.0, 0.25, 0.5, 0.75, 1.0]~

Similar to ~np.linspace~

functions.name: py5canvas.globals.rotate_vector
documentation:
Rotate a 2D vector (x, y) by a given angle in radians.
Input can be two numbers ~x, y~ or a tuple/array, followed by the angle in radians

functions.name: py5canvas.globals.dist
documentation: Computes the (Euclidean) distance between two points

functions.name: py5canvas.globals.mag
documentation:
Returns the magnitude (length) of a vector.
Accepts one vector as an argument or a sequenc of coordinates for each component of the vector

functions.name: py5canvas.globals.heading
documentation: Returns the heading (orientation) in radians of a 2d vector

functions.name: py5canvas.globals.direction
documentation: Returns a vector with magnitude 1 and oriented according to an angle specified in radians

functions.name: py5canvas.globals.remap
documentation: Re-maps a number from one range to another.

functions.name: py5canvas.globals.load_image
documentation: Load an image from disk. Actually returns a PIL image

functions.name: py5canvas.globals.bezier_point
documentation:
Get a point along a bezier curve (cubic) given a parameter value

Arguments:
- Four points, specified either as a list of points, a sequence of four points, or a sequence of coordiantes
- ~t~ the parameter at which to sample the curve. This can also be an array, in which case the result will be a list of tangents

functions.name: py5canvas.globals.bezier_tangent
documentation:
Get the tangent to a bezier curve (cubic) given a parameter value

Arguments:
- Four points, specified either as a list of points, a sequence of four points, or a sequence of coordiantes
- ~t~ the parameter at which to sample the curve. This can also be an array, in which case the result will be a list of tangents

modules.name: py5canvas.create_dummy_globals
documentation:
Creates a module file `dummy_globals.py` that is used to trick a Python linter
into knowing the canvas globals that are injected

modules.name: py5canvas.dummy_globals
documentation: Autogenerated canvas/sketch methods as globals to trick the linter

functions.name: py5canvas.dummy_globals.set_color_scale
documentation:
Set color scale:

Arguments:

- ~scale~ (float): the color scale. if we want to specify colors in the ~0...255~ range,
 ~scale~ will be ~255~. If we want to specify colors in the ~0...1~ range, ~scale~ will be ~1~

functions.name: py5canvas.dummy_globals.color_mode
documentation:
Set the color mode for the canvas

Arguments:

- ~mode~ (string): can be one of 'rgb', 'hsv' depending on the desired color mode
- ~scale~ (float): the scale for the color values (e.g. 255 for 0...255 range, 1 for 0...1 range)

Examples:

- ~color_mode('rgb', 1.0)~ will set the color mode to RGB in the 0-1 range.

functions.name: py5canvas.dummy_globals.fill
documentation:
Set the color of the current fill

Arguments:

- A single argument specifies a grayscale value, e.g ~fill(128)~ will fill with 50% gray.
- Two arguments specify grayscale with opacity, e.g. ~fill(255, 128)~ will fill with transparent white.
- Three arguments specify a color depending on the color mode (rgb or hsv)
- Four arguments specify a color with opacity

functions.name: py5canvas.dummy_globals.stroke
documentation:
Set the color of the current stroke

Arguments:
- A single argument specifies a grayscale value, e.g. ~stroke(255)~ will set the stroke to white.
- Two arguments specify grayscale with opacity, e.g. ~stroke(0, 128)~ will set the stroke to black with 50% opacity.
- Three arguments specify a color depending on the color mode (rgb or hsv), e.g. ~stroke(255, 0, 0)~ will set the stroke to red, when the color mode is RGB
- Four arguments specify a color with opacity

functions.name: py5canvas.dummy_globals.stroke_weight
documentation:
Set the line width

Arguments:
- The width in pixel of the stroke

functions.name: py5canvas.dummy_globals.line_join
documentation:
Specify the 'join' mode for polylines.

Arguments:

- ~join~ (string): can be one of "miter", "bevel" or "round"

functions.name: py5canvas.dummy_globals.blend_mode
documentation:
Specify the blending mode

Arguments:

- ~mode~ (string) can be one of: "clear", "source", "over", "in", "out", "atop",
  "dest", "dest_over", "dest_in", "dest_out", "dest_atop", "xor", "add", "saturate", "multiply", "screen", "overlay", "darken", "lighten", "color_dodge", "color_burn", "hard_light", "soft_light", "difference", "exclusion", "hsl_hue", "hsl_saturation", "hsl_color", "hsl_luminosity"
  See [[https://www.cairographics.org/operators/]] for a discussion on the different operators.

functions.name: py5canvas.dummy_globals.line_cap
documentation:
Specify the 'cap' for lines.

Arguments:

- ~cap~ (string): can be one of "butt", "round" or "square"

functions.name: py5canvas.dummy_globals.text_align
documentation:
Specify the text alignment

Arguments:
- ~halign~ (string): Horizontal alignment. One of "left", "center" or "right"
- ~valign~ (string): Horizontal alignment. One of "bottom" (default), "top" or "center"

functions.name: py5canvas.dummy_globals.text_size
documentation:
Specify the text size

Arguments:

- ~size~ (int): the text size

functions.name: py5canvas.dummy_globals.text_font
documentation:
Specify the font to use for text rendering
Arguments:

- ~font~ (string): the name of a system font

functions.name: py5canvas.dummy_globals.pop_style
documentation: Restore the previously pushed drawing state

functions.name: py5canvas.dummy_globals.push
documentation: Save the current drawing state and transformations

functions.name: py5canvas.dummy_globals.pop
documentation: Restore the previously pushed drawing state and transformations

functions.name: py5canvas.dummy_globals.translate
documentation:
Translate by specifying ~x~ and ~y~ offset.

Arguments:

- The offset can be specified as an array/list (e.g ~translate([x,y])~
  or as single arguments (e.g. ~translate(x, y)~)

functions.name: py5canvas.dummy_globals.scale
documentation:
Apply a scaling transformation.

Arguments:

- Providing a single number will apply a uniform transformation.
- Providing a pair of number will scale in the x and y directions.
- The scale can be specified as an array/list (e.g ~scale([x,y])~
or as single arguments (e.g. ~scale(x, y)~)'''

functions.name: py5canvas.dummy_globals.rect_mode
documentation:
Set the "mode" for drawing rectangles.

Arguments:
- ~mode~ (string): can be one of 'corner', 'corners', 'center', 'radius'

functions.name: py5canvas.dummy_globals.ellipse_mode
documentation:
Set the "mode" for drawing rectangles.

Arguments:
- ~mode~ (string): can be one of 'corner', 'center'

functions.name: py5canvas.dummy_globals.rectangle
documentation:
Draw a rectangle.
Can use ~rect~ equivalently.

Arguments:
The first sequence of arguments is one of

 - ~[x, y], [width, height]~,
 - ~[x, y], width, height~,
 - ~x, y, width, height~
 - ~[[topleft_x, topleft_y], [bottomright_x, bottomright_y]]~

The last option will ignore the rect mode since it explictly defines the corners of the rect

The interpretation of ~x~ and ~y~ depends on the current rect mode. These indicate the
center of the rectangle if the rect mode is ~"center"~ and the top left corner otherwise.

functions.name: py5canvas.dummy_globals.square
documentation:
Draw a square.

Arguments:

The first sequence of arguments is one of
 - ~[x, y], size~,
 - ~x, y, size~

The interpretation of ~x~ and ~y~ depends on the current rect mode. These indicate the
center of the rectangle if the rect mode is ~"center"~ and the top left corner otherwise.

functions.name: py5canvas.dummy_globals.rect
documentation:
Draws a rectangle.

Input arguments can be in the following formats:

 - ~[topleft_x, topleft_y], [width, height]~,
 - ~[topleft_x, topleft_y], width, height~,
 - ~topleft_x, topleft_y, width, height~

Depending on

functions.name: py5canvas.dummy_globals.quad
documentation:
Draws a quadrangle given four points

Input arguments can be in the following formats:

 - ~a, b, c, d~ (Four points specified as lists/tuples/numpy arrays
 - ~x1, y1, x2, y2, x3, y3, x4, y4~, a sequence of numbers, one for each coordinate

functions.name: py5canvas.dummy_globals.line
documentation:
Draws a line between two points

Input arguments can be in the following formats:

 - ~a, b~ (Two points specified as lists/tuples/numpy arrays
 - ~x1, y1, x2, y2~, a sequence of numbers, one for each coordinate

functions.name: py5canvas.dummy_globals.point
documentation:
Draw a point at a given position

Input arguments can be in the following formats:

 - ~[x, y]~: a single point specified as a tuple/list/numpy array
 - ~x1, y1~: two coordinates

functions.name: py5canvas.dummy_globals.arrow
documentation:
Draw an arrow between two points

Input arguments can be in the following formats:

 - ~a, b~ (Two points specified as lists/tuples/numpy arrays
 - ~x1, y1, x2, y2~, a sequence of numbers, one for each coordinate

functions.name: py5canvas.dummy_globals.triangle
documentation:
Draws a triangle given three points

Input arguments can be in the following formats:

 - ~a, b, c~ (Four points specified as lists/tuples/numpy arrays
 - ~x1, y1, x2, y2, x3, y3~

functions.name: py5canvas.dummy_globals.circle
documentation:
Draw a circle given center and radius

Input arguments can be in the following formats:

- ~[center_x, center_y], radius~,
- ~center_x, center_y, raidus~

functions.name: py5canvas.dummy_globals.ellipse
documentation:
Draw an ellipse with center, width and height.

Input arguments can be in the following formats:

- ~[center_x, center_y], [width, height]~,
- ~[center_x, center_y], width, height~,
- ~center_x, center_y, width, height~

functions.name: py5canvas.dummy_globals.arc
documentation:
Draw an arc given the center of the ellipse ~x, y~
the size of the ellipse ~w, h~ and the initial and final angles
in radians  ~start, stop~.
NB. this differs from Processing/P5js as it always draws

Input arguments can be in the following formats:

  - ~x, y, w, h, start, stop~
  - ~[x, y]~, ~[w, h]~, ~[start, stop]~
  - ~[x, y]~, ~w, h, start, stop~

functions.name: py5canvas.dummy_globals.end_contour
documentation:
End drawing a contour

Arguments:

- ~close~ (bool, optional): if ~True~ close the contour

functions.name: py5canvas.dummy_globals.vertex
documentation:
Add a vertex to current contour

Input arguments can be in the following formats:

- ~[x, y]~
- ~x, y~

functions.name: py5canvas.dummy_globals.curve_vertex
documentation:
Add a curved vertex to current contour

Input arguments can be in the following formats:

- ~[x, y]~
- ~x, y~

functions.name: py5canvas.dummy_globals.bezier_vertex
documentation:
Draw a cubic Bezier segment from the current point
requires a first control point to be already defined with ~vertex~.


Requires three points. Input arguments can be in the following formats:

- ~[x1, y1], [x2, y2], [x3, y3]~
- ~x1, y1, x2, y2, x3, y3~

functions.name: py5canvas.dummy_globals.curve_tightness
documentation: Sets the 'tension' parameter for the curve used when using ~curve_vertex~

functions.name: py5canvas.dummy_globals.cubic
documentation:
Draw a cubic bezier curve

Input arguments can be in the following formats:

- ~[x1, y1], [x2, y2], [x3, y3]~
- ~x1, y1, x2, y2, x3, y3~

functions.name: py5canvas.dummy_globals.quadratic
documentation:
Draw a quadratic bezier curve

Input arguments can be in the following formats:

-    ~[x1, y1], [x2, y2]~
-    ~x1, y1, x2, y2~

functions.name: py5canvas.dummy_globals.bezier
documentation:
Draws a bezier curve segment from current point
    The degree of the curve (2 or 3) depends on the input arguments
Arguments:
Input arguments can be in the following formats:
    ~[x1, y1], [x2, y2], [x3, y3]~ is cubic
    ~x1, y1, x2, y2, x3, y3~ is cubic
    ~[x1, y1], [x2, y2]~ is quadratic
    ~x1, y1, x2, y2~ is quadratic

functions.name: py5canvas.dummy_globals.create_graphics
documentation:
Create a new canvas with the specified width and height
E.g. ~c = create_graphics(128, 128)~ will put a new canvas into
the variable ~c~. You can draw the contents of the canvas with the ~image~ function.

functions.name: py5canvas.dummy_globals.image
documentation:
Draw an image at position with (optional) size and (optional) opacity

Arguments:

- ~img~: The input image. Can be either a PIL image, a numpy array, a Canvas or a pyCairo surface.
- optional arguments: position and size can be specified with the following formats:
    - ~x, y~:  position only
    - ~x, y, w, h~: position and size
    - ~[x, y]~: position only (also a numpy array or tuple are valid)
    - ~[x, y], [w, h]~: position and size
if the position is not specified, the original image dimensions will be used

- ~opacity~: a value between 0 and 1 specifying image opacity.

functions.name: py5canvas.dummy_globals.shape
documentation:
Draw a shape represented as a list of polylines, see the ~polyline~
method for the format of each polyline

functions.name: py5canvas.dummy_globals.text
documentation:
Draw text at a given position

Arguments:

    - ~text`, the text to display
    - the position of the text, either a pair of x, y arguments or a list like object (e.g. ~[x, y]~)
    - ~align~, horizontal alignment, etiher ~'left'~ (default), ~'center'~ or ~'right'~
    - ~valign~, vertical alignment, etiher ~'bottom'~ (default), ~'center'~ or ~'top'~
    (Deprecated) if center=True the text will be horizontally centered

functions.name: py5canvas.dummy_globals.text_shape
documentation:
Retrieves polylines for a given string of text in the current font

Arguments:

- ~text`, the text to sample
- the position of the text, either a pair of x, y arguments or a list like object (e.g. ~[x, y]~)
- ~dist~, approximate distance between samples
- ~align~, horizontal alignment, etiher ~'left'~ (default), ~'center'~ or ~'right'~
- ~valign~, vertical alignment, etiher ~'bottom'~ (default), ~'center'~ or ~'top'~

functions.name: py5canvas.dummy_globals.text_points
documentation:
Retrieves points for a given string of text in the current font

Arguments:

- ~text`, the text to sample
- the position of the text, either a pair of x, y arguments or a list like object (e.g. ~[x, y]~)
- ~dist~, approximate distance between samples
- ~align~ (named), horizontal alignment, etiher ~'left'~ (default), ~'center'~ or ~'right'~
- ~valign~ (named), vertical alignment, etiher ~'bottom'~ (default), ~'center'~ or ~'top'~

functions.name: py5canvas.dummy_globals.text_bounds
documentation: Returns the bounding box of a string of text at a given position

functions.name: py5canvas.dummy_globals.polygon
documentation:
Draw a polygon (closed by default).

The polyline is specified as either:

- a list of ~[x,y]~ pairs (e.g. ~[[0, 100], [200, 100], [200, 200]]~)
- a numpy array with shape ~(n, 2)~, representing ~n~ points (a point for each row and a coordinate for each column)
- two lists (or numpy array) of numbers, one for each coordinate

To create an opne polygon set the named ~close~ argument to ~False~, e.g. ~c.polygon(points, close=False)~.

functions.name: py5canvas.dummy_globals.curve
documentation:
Draw a curve (open by default).

The polyline is specified as either:

- a list of ~[x,y]~ pairs (e.g. ~[[0, 100], [200, 100], [200, 200]]~)
- a numpy array with shape ~(n, 2)~, representing ~n~ points (a point for each row and a coordinate for each column)
- two lists (or numpy array) of numbers, one for each coordinate

To close the curve set the named ~close~ argument to ~True~, e.g. ~c.curve(points, close=True)~.

functions.name: py5canvas.dummy_globals.polyline
documentation:
Draw a polyline (open by default).

The polyline is specified as either:

- a list of ~[x,y]~ pairs (e.g. ~[[0, 100], [200, 100], [200, 200]]~)
- a numpy array with shape ~(n, 2)~, representing ~n~ points (a point for each row and a coordinate for each column)
- two lists (or numpy array) of numbers, one for each coordinate

To close the polyline set the named ~close~ argument to ~True~, e.g. ~c.polyline(points, close=True)~.

functions.name: py5canvas.dummy_globals.copy
documentation:
The first parameter can optionally be an image, if an image is not specified the funtion will use
the canvas image, .
The next four parameters, sx, sy, sw, and sh determine the region to copy from the source image.
(sx, sy) is the top-left corner of the region. sw and sh are the region's width and height.
The next four parameters, dx, dy, dw, and dh determine the region of the canvas to copy into.
(dx, dy) is the top-left corner of the region. dw and dh are the region's width and height.

~copy(src_image, sx, sy, sw, sh, dx, dy, dw, dh)~
or
~copy(sx, sy, sw, sh, dx, dy, dw, dh)~

functions.name: py5canvas.dummy_globals.background
documentation:
Clear the canvas with a given color
Accepts either an array with the color components, or single color components (as in ~fill~)

functions.name: py5canvas.dummy_globals.get_image
documentation: Get canvas image as a numpy array

functions.name: py5canvas.dummy_globals.get_image_grayscale
documentation: Returns the canvas image as a grayscale numpy array (in 0-1 range)

functions.name: py5canvas.dummy_globals.save_image
documentation:
Save the canvas to an image

Arguments:

- The path where to save

functions.name: py5canvas.dummy_globals.save_svg
documentation:
Save the canvas to an svg file

Arguments:

- The path where to save

functions.name: py5canvas.dummy_globals.save_pdf
documentation:
Save the canvas to an svg file

Arguments:

- The path where to save

functions.name: py5canvas.dummy_globals.save
documentation:
Save the canvas into a given file path
The file format depends on the file extension

functions.name: py5canvas.dummy_globals.show
documentation: Display the canvas in a notebook

functions.name: py5canvas.dummy_globals.show_plt
documentation:
Show the canvas in a notebook with matplotlib

Arguments:

- ~size~ (tuple, optional): The size of the displayed image, by default this is the size of the canvas
- ~title~ (string, optional): A title for the figure
- ~axis~ (bool, optional): If ~True~ shows the coordinate axes

functions.name: py5canvas.dummy_globals.open_file_dialog
documentation:
Opens a dialog to select a file to be opened,
the first argument is the extension or the file to be opened,
e.g. ~'png'~ or a list of extensions, e.g. ~['png', 'jpg']

The function returns the path of the file if it is selected or an empty string othewise.

functions.name: py5canvas.dummy_globals.save_file_dialog
documentation:
Opens a dialog to select a file to be saved,
the first argument is the extension or the file to be saved,
e.g. ~'png'~ or a list of extensions, e.g. ~['png', 'jpg']

The function returns the path of the file if it is selected or an empty string othewise.

functions.name: py5canvas.dummy_globals.open_folder_dialog
documentation:
Opens a dialog to select a folder/directory to be opened,

The function returns the path of the directory if it is selected or an empty string othewise.

functions.name: py5canvas.dummy_globals.save_canvas
documentation: Tells the sketch to dump the next frame to an SVG file

functions.name: py5canvas.dummy_globals.toggle_fullscreen
documentation: Toggle between fullscreen and windowed mode

functions.name: py5canvas.dummy_globals.fullscreen
documentation: Sets fullscreen or windowed mode depending on the first argument (~True~ or ~False~)

functions.name: py5canvas.dummy_globals.no_loop
documentation: Stops the drawing loop keeping the last frame fixed on the canvas

functions.name: py5canvas.dummy_globals.grab_image_sequence
documentation:
Saves a sequence of image files to a directory, one for each frame.
By default this will reload the current script.

Arguments:
- ~path~ (string), the directory where to save the images
- ~num_frames~ (int), the number of frames to save

functions.name: py5canvas.dummy_globals.grab_movie
documentation:
Saves a mp4 movie from a number of frames to a specified path.
By default this will reload the current script.

Arguments:
- ~path~ (string), the directory where to save the video
- ~num_frames~ (int), the number of frames to save

functions.name: py5canvas.dummy_globals.title
documentation: Sets the title of the sketch window

functions.name: py5canvas.dummy_globals.frame_rate
documentation: Set the framerate of the sketch in frames-per-second

modules.name: py5canvas.canvas
documentation:
```
             eeeee
eeeee e    e 8     eeee eeeee eeeee ee   e eeeee eeeee
8   8 8    8 8eeee 8  8 8   8 8   8 88   8 8   8 8   "
8eee8 8eeee8     8 8e   8eee8 8e  8 88  e8 8eee8 8eeee
88      88       8 88   88  8 88  8  8  8  88  8    88
88      88   eeee8 88e8 88  8 88  8  8ee8  88  8 8ee88

```
Simplistic utilty to mimic [P5js](https://p5js.org) in Python/Jupyter notebooks.
© Daniel Berio (@colormotor) 2023 - ...

functions.name: py5canvas.canvas.radians
documentation: Get radians given an angle in degrees

functions.name: py5canvas.canvas.degrees
documentation: Get degrees given an angle in radians

functions.name: py5canvas.canvas.numpy_to_surface
documentation: Convert numpy array to a pycairo surface

functions.name: py5canvas.canvas.show_images
documentation: Display multiple images in a grid

functions.name: py5canvas.canvas.cardinal_spline
documentation:
Returns a Bezier chain for a Cardinal spline interpolation for a sequence of values
c is the tension parameter with 0.5 a Catmull-Rom spline

functions.name: py5canvas.canvas.eval_bezier
documentation: Bezier curve of degree len(P)-1. d is the derivative order (0 gives positions)

functions.name: py5canvas.canvas.fix_namespace
documentation: RecordingSurface adds a clip-path attribute that breaks Illustrator import

functions.name: py5canvas.canvas.noise_seed
documentation: Sets the seed for the noise generator

functions.name: py5canvas.canvas.noise_detail
documentation:
Adjusts the character and level of detail produced by the Perlin noise function.

Arguments:

- ~octaves~ (int): the number of noise 'octaves'. Each octave has double the frequency of the previous.
- ~falloff~ (float, default 0.5): a number between 0 and 1 that multiplies the amplitude of each consectutive octave

functions.name: py5canvas.canvas.noise
documentation:
Returns a Perlin noise value (between 0 and 1) at a given coordinate.
Noise is created by summing consecutive "octaves" with increasing level of detail.
Each octave has double the frequency of the previous and an amplitude falls off for each octave. By default the falloff is 0.5.
The default number of octaves is ~4~. Use `noise_detail~ to set the number of octaves and falloff.

Arguments:

- The arguments to this function can vary from 1 to 3, determining the "space" that is sampled to generate noise.
The function also accepts a single array parameter with 1 to 3 elements.

classes.name: py5canvas.canvas.MultiContext
documentation:
Workaround for TeeSurface not working on Mac (at least)
This should enable rendering to multiple surfaces (each with their own context)

classes.name: py5canvas.canvas.Canvas

methods.name: py5canvas.canvas.Canvas.set_color_scale
documentation:
Set color scale:

Arguments:

- ~scale~ (float): the color scale. if we want to specify colors in the ~0...255~ range,
 ~scale~ will be ~255~. If we want to specify colors in the ~0...1~ range, ~scale~ will be ~1~

methods.name: py5canvas.canvas.Canvas._get_stroke_or_fill_color
documentation:
Returns the current stroke color if set, the fill color otherwise
returns the current stroke or fill color as a numpy array, or ~None~ if no color is set

methods.name: py5canvas.canvas.Canvas.center
documentation: The center of the canvas (as a 2d numpy array)

methods.name: py5canvas.canvas.Canvas.color_mode
documentation:
Set the color mode for the canvas

Arguments:

- ~mode~ (string): can be one of 'rgb', 'hsv' depending on the desired color mode
- ~scale~ (float): the scale for the color values (e.g. 255 for 0...255 range, 1 for 0...1 range)

Examples:

- ~color_mode('rgb', 1.0)~ will set the color mode to RGB in the 0-1 range.

methods.name: py5canvas.canvas.Canvas.fill
documentation:
Set the color of the current fill

Arguments:

- A single argument specifies a grayscale value, e.g ~fill(128)~ will fill with 50% gray.
- Two arguments specify grayscale with opacity, e.g. ~fill(255, 128)~ will fill with transparent white.
- Three arguments specify a color depending on the color mode (rgb or hsv)
- Four arguments specify a color with opacity

methods.name: py5canvas.canvas.Canvas.stroke
documentation:
Set the color of the current stroke

Arguments:
- A single argument specifies a grayscale value, e.g. ~stroke(255)~ will set the stroke to white.
- Two arguments specify grayscale with opacity, e.g. ~stroke(0, 128)~ will set the stroke to black with 50% opacity.
- Three arguments specify a color depending on the color mode (rgb or hsv), e.g. ~stroke(255, 0, 0)~ will set the stroke to red, when the color mode is RGB
- Four arguments specify a color with opacity

methods.name: py5canvas.canvas.Canvas.stroke_weight
documentation:
Set the line width

Arguments:
- The width in pixel of the stroke

methods.name: py5canvas.canvas.Canvas.line_join
documentation:
Specify the 'join' mode for polylines.

Arguments:

- ~join~ (string): can be one of "miter", "bevel" or "round"

methods.name: py5canvas.canvas.Canvas.blend_mode
documentation:
Specify the blending mode

Arguments:

- ~mode~ (string) can be one of: "clear", "source", "over", "in", "out", "atop",
  "dest", "dest_over", "dest_in", "dest_out", "dest_atop", "xor", "add", "saturate", "multiply", "screen", "overlay", "darken", "lighten", "color_dodge", "color_burn", "hard_light", "soft_light", "difference", "exclusion", "hsl_hue", "hsl_saturation", "hsl_color", "hsl_luminosity"
  See [[https://www.cairographics.org/operators/]] for a discussion on the different operators.

methods.name: py5canvas.canvas.Canvas.line_cap
documentation:
Specify the 'cap' for lines.

Arguments:

- ~cap~ (string): can be one of "butt", "round" or "square"

methods.name: py5canvas.canvas.Canvas.text_align
documentation:
Specify the text alignment

Arguments:
- ~halign~ (string): Horizontal alignment. One of "left", "center" or "right"
- ~valign~ (string): Horizontal alignment. One of "bottom" (default), "top" or "center"

methods.name: py5canvas.canvas.Canvas.text_size
documentation:
Specify the text size

Arguments:

- ~size~ (int): the text size

methods.name: py5canvas.canvas.Canvas.text_font
documentation:
Specify the font to use for text rendering
Arguments:

- ~font~ (string): the name of a system font

methods.name: py5canvas.canvas.Canvas.pop_style
documentation: Restore the previously pushed drawing state

methods.name: py5canvas.canvas.Canvas.push
documentation: Save the current drawing state and transformations

methods.name: py5canvas.canvas.Canvas.pop
documentation: Restore the previously pushed drawing state and transformations

methods.name: py5canvas.canvas.Canvas.translate
documentation:
Translate by specifying ~x~ and ~y~ offset.

Arguments:

- The offset can be specified as an array/list (e.g ~translate([x,y])~
  or as single arguments (e.g. ~translate(x, y)~)

methods.name: py5canvas.canvas.Canvas.scale
documentation:
Apply a scaling transformation.

Arguments:

- Providing a single number will apply a uniform transformation.
- Providing a pair of number will scale in the x and y directions.
- The scale can be specified as an array/list (e.g ~scale([x,y])~
or as single arguments (e.g. ~scale(x, y)~)'''

methods.name: py5canvas.canvas.Canvas.rect_mode
documentation:
Set the "mode" for drawing rectangles.

Arguments:
- ~mode~ (string): can be one of 'corner', 'corners', 'center', 'radius'

methods.name: py5canvas.canvas.Canvas.ellipse_mode
documentation:
Set the "mode" for drawing rectangles.

Arguments:
- ~mode~ (string): can be one of 'corner', 'center'

methods.name: py5canvas.canvas.Canvas.rectangle
documentation:
Draw a rectangle.
Can use ~rect~ equivalently.

Arguments:
The first sequence of arguments is one of

 - ~[x, y], [width, height]~,
 - ~[x, y], width, height~,
 - ~x, y, width, height~
 - ~[[topleft_x, topleft_y], [bottomright_x, bottomright_y]]~

The last option will ignore the rect mode since it explictly defines the corners of the rect

The interpretation of ~x~ and ~y~ depends on the current rect mode. These indicate the
center of the rectangle if the rect mode is ~"center"~ and the top left corner otherwise.

methods.name: py5canvas.canvas.Canvas.square
documentation:
Draw a square.

Arguments:

The first sequence of arguments is one of
 - ~[x, y], size~,
 - ~x, y, size~

The interpretation of ~x~ and ~y~ depends on the current rect mode. These indicate the
center of the rectangle if the rect mode is ~"center"~ and the top left corner otherwise.

methods.name: py5canvas.canvas.Canvas.rect
documentation:
Draws a rectangle.

Input arguments can be in the following formats:

 - ~[topleft_x, topleft_y], [width, height]~,
 - ~[topleft_x, topleft_y], width, height~,
 - ~topleft_x, topleft_y, width, height~

Depending on

methods.name: py5canvas.canvas.Canvas.quad
documentation:
Draws a quadrangle given four points

Input arguments can be in the following formats:

 - ~a, b, c, d~ (Four points specified as lists/tuples/numpy arrays
 - ~x1, y1, x2, y2, x3, y3, x4, y4~, a sequence of numbers, one for each coordinate

methods.name: py5canvas.canvas.Canvas.line
documentation:
Draws a line between two points

Input arguments can be in the following formats:

 - ~a, b~ (Two points specified as lists/tuples/numpy arrays
 - ~x1, y1, x2, y2~, a sequence of numbers, one for each coordinate

methods.name: py5canvas.canvas.Canvas.point
documentation:
Draw a point at a given position

Input arguments can be in the following formats:

 - ~[x, y]~: a single point specified as a tuple/list/numpy array
 - ~x1, y1~: two coordinates

methods.name: py5canvas.canvas.Canvas.arrow
documentation:
Draw an arrow between two points

Input arguments can be in the following formats:

 - ~a, b~ (Two points specified as lists/tuples/numpy arrays
 - ~x1, y1, x2, y2~, a sequence of numbers, one for each coordinate

methods.name: py5canvas.canvas.Canvas.triangle
documentation:
Draws a triangle given three points

Input arguments can be in the following formats:

 - ~a, b, c~ (Four points specified as lists/tuples/numpy arrays
 - ~x1, y1, x2, y2, x3, y3~

methods.name: py5canvas.canvas.Canvas.circle
documentation:
Draw a circle given center and radius

Input arguments can be in the following formats:

- ~[center_x, center_y], radius~,
- ~center_x, center_y, raidus~

methods.name: py5canvas.canvas.Canvas.ellipse
documentation:
Draw an ellipse with center, width and height.

Input arguments can be in the following formats:

- ~[center_x, center_y], [width, height]~,
- ~[center_x, center_y], width, height~,
- ~center_x, center_y, width, height~

methods.name: py5canvas.canvas.Canvas.arc
documentation:
Draw an arc given the center of the ellipse ~x, y~
the size of the ellipse ~w, h~ and the initial and final angles
in radians  ~start, stop~.
NB. this differs from Processing/P5js as it always draws

Input arguments can be in the following formats:

  - ~x, y, w, h, start, stop~
  - ~[x, y]~, ~[w, h]~, ~[start, stop]~
  - ~[x, y]~, ~w, h, start, stop~

methods.name: py5canvas.canvas.Canvas.end_contour
documentation:
End drawing a contour

Arguments:

- ~close~ (bool, optional): if ~True~ close the contour

methods.name: py5canvas.canvas.Canvas.vertex
documentation:
Add a vertex to current contour

Input arguments can be in the following formats:

- ~[x, y]~
- ~x, y~

methods.name: py5canvas.canvas.Canvas.curve_vertex
documentation:
Add a curved vertex to current contour

Input arguments can be in the following formats:

- ~[x, y]~
- ~x, y~

methods.name: py5canvas.canvas.Canvas.bezier_vertex
documentation:
Draw a cubic Bezier segment from the current point
requires a first control point to be already defined with ~vertex~.


Requires three points. Input arguments can be in the following formats:

- ~[x1, y1], [x2, y2], [x3, y3]~
- ~x1, y1, x2, y2, x3, y3~

methods.name: py5canvas.canvas.Canvas.curve_tightness
documentation: Sets the 'tension' parameter for the curve used when using ~curve_vertex~

methods.name: py5canvas.canvas.Canvas.cubic
documentation:
Draw a cubic bezier curve

Input arguments can be in the following formats:

- ~[x1, y1], [x2, y2], [x3, y3]~
- ~x1, y1, x2, y2, x3, y3~
''' Add a vertex to current contour
Input arguments can be in the following formats:

methods.name: py5canvas.canvas.Canvas.quadratic
documentation:
Draw a quadratic bezier curve

Input arguments can be in the following formats:

-    ~[x1, y1], [x2, y2]~
-    ~x1, y1, x2, y2~

methods.name: py5canvas.canvas.Canvas.bezier
documentation:
Draws a bezier curve segment from current point
    The degree of the curve (2 or 3) depends on the input arguments
Arguments:
Input arguments can be in the following formats:
    ~[x1, y1], [x2, y2], [x3, y3]~ is cubic
    ~x1, y1, x2, y2, x3, y3~ is cubic
    ~[x1, y1], [x2, y2]~ is quadratic
    ~x1, y1, x2, y2~ is quadratic
def quadratic_to_cubic(self, x1, y1, x2, y2):
''' Convert a quadratic bezier curve to a cubic bezier curve
Input arguments can be in the following formats:

methods.name: py5canvas.canvas.Canvas.create_graphics
documentation:
Create a new canvas with the specified width and height
E.g. ~c = create_graphics(128, 128)~ will put a new canvas into
the variable ~c~. You can draw the contents of the canvas with the ~image~ function.

methods.name: py5canvas.canvas.Canvas.image
documentation:
Draw an image at position with (optional) size and (optional) opacity

Arguments:

- ~img~: The input image. Can be either a PIL image, a numpy array, a Canvas or a pyCairo surface.
- optional arguments: position and size can be specified with the following formats:
    - ~x, y~:  position only
    - ~x, y, w, h~: position and size
    - ~[x, y]~: position only (also a numpy array or tuple are valid)
    - ~[x, y], [w, h]~: position and size
if the position is not specified, the original image dimensions will be used

- ~opacity~: a value between 0 and 1 specifying image opacity.

methods.name: py5canvas.canvas.Canvas.shape
documentation:
Draw a shape represented as a list of polylines, see the ~polyline~
method for the format of each polyline

methods.name: py5canvas.canvas.Canvas.text
documentation:
Draw text at a given position

Arguments:

    - ~text`, the text to display
    - the position of the text, either a pair of x, y arguments or a list like object (e.g. ~[x, y]~)
    - ~align~, horizontal alignment, etiher ~'left'~ (default), ~'center'~ or ~'right'~
    - ~valign~, vertical alignment, etiher ~'bottom'~ (default), ~'center'~ or ~'top'~
    (Deprecated) if center=True the text will be horizontally centered

methods.name: py5canvas.canvas.Canvas.text_shape
documentation:
Retrieves polylines for a given string of text in the current font

Arguments:

- ~text`, the text to sample
- the position of the text, either a pair of x, y arguments or a list like object (e.g. ~[x, y]~)
- ~dist~, approximate distance between samples
- ~align~, horizontal alignment, etiher ~'left'~ (default), ~'center'~ or ~'right'~
- ~valign~, vertical alignment, etiher ~'bottom'~ (default), ~'center'~ or ~'top'~

methods.name: py5canvas.canvas.Canvas.text_points
documentation:
Retrieves points for a given string of text in the current font

Arguments:

- ~text`, the text to sample
- the position of the text, either a pair of x, y arguments or a list like object (e.g. ~[x, y]~)
- ~dist~, approximate distance between samples
- ~align~ (named), horizontal alignment, etiher ~'left'~ (default), ~'center'~ or ~'right'~
- ~valign~ (named), vertical alignment, etiher ~'bottom'~ (default), ~'center'~ or ~'top'~

methods.name: py5canvas.canvas.Canvas.text_bounds
documentation: Returns the bounding box of a string of text at a given position

methods.name: py5canvas.canvas.Canvas.polygon
documentation:
Draw a polygon (closed by default).

The polyline is specified as either:

- a list of ~[x,y]~ pairs (e.g. ~[[0, 100], [200, 100], [200, 200]]~)
- a numpy array with shape ~(n, 2)~, representing ~n~ points (a point for each row and a coordinate for each column)
- two lists (or numpy array) of numbers, one for each coordinate

To create an opne polygon set the named ~close~ argument to ~False~, e.g. ~c.polygon(points, close=False)~.

methods.name: py5canvas.canvas.Canvas.curve
documentation:
Draw a curve (open by default).

The polyline is specified as either:

- a list of ~[x,y]~ pairs (e.g. ~[[0, 100], [200, 100], [200, 200]]~)
- a numpy array with shape ~(n, 2)~, representing ~n~ points (a point for each row and a coordinate for each column)
- two lists (or numpy array) of numbers, one for each coordinate

To close the curve set the named ~close~ argument to ~True~, e.g. ~c.curve(points, close=True)~.

methods.name: py5canvas.canvas.Canvas.polyline
documentation:
Draw a polyline (open by default).

The polyline is specified as either:

- a list of ~[x,y]~ pairs (e.g. ~[[0, 100], [200, 100], [200, 200]]~)
- a numpy array with shape ~(n, 2)~, representing ~n~ points (a point for each row and a coordinate for each column)
- two lists (or numpy array) of numbers, one for each coordinate

To close the polyline set the named ~close~ argument to ~True~, e.g. ~c.polyline(points, close=True)~.

methods.name: py5canvas.canvas.Canvas.copy
documentation:
The first parameter can optionally be an image, if an image is not specified the funtion will use
the canvas image, .
The next four parameters, sx, sy, sw, and sh determine the region to copy from the source image.
(sx, sy) is the top-left corner of the region. sw and sh are the region's width and height.
The next four parameters, dx, dy, dw, and dh determine the region of the canvas to copy into.
(dx, dy) is the top-left corner of the region. dw and dh are the region's width and height.

~copy(src_image, sx, sy, sw, sh, dx, dy, dw, dh)~
or
~copy(sx, sy, sw, sh, dx, dy, dw, dh)~

Accepts either an array with the color components, or single color components (as in ~fill~)

Save the canvas into a given file path
The file format depends on the file extension
''' Save the canvas to an image'''

methods.name: py5canvas.canvas.Canvas.show
documentation: Display the canvas in a notebook

methods.name: py5canvas.canvas.Canvas.show_plt
documentation:
Show the canvas in a notebook with matplotlib

Arguments:

- ~size~ (tuple, optional): The size of the displayed image, by default this is the size of the canvas
- ~title~ (string, optional): A title for the figure
- ~axis~ (bool, optional): If ~True~ shows the coordinate axes

Defines a drawing canvas (pyCairo) that behaves similarly to p5js

Constructor arguments:

- ~width~ : (~int~), width of the canvas in pixels
- ~height~ : (~int~), height of the canvas in pixels
- ~clear_callback~ (optional): function, a callback to be called when the canvas is cleared (for internal use mostly)

In a notebook you can create a canvas globally with either of:

- ~size(width, height)~
- ~create_canvas(width, height)~

When using these functions all the canvas functionalities below will become globally available to the notebook.

classes.name: py5canvas.canvas.VideoInput
documentation:
Video Input utility (requires OpenCV to be installed).
Allows for reading frames from a video file or camera.

Arguments:

- ~name~: Either an integer indicating the device number, or a string indicating the path of a video file
- ~size~: A tuple indicating the desired size of the video frames (width, height)
- ~resize_mode~: A string indicating the desired resize mode. Can be 'crop' or 'stretch'

modules.name: py5canvas.run_sketch_pyopengl
documentation:
```
             eeeee
eeeee e    e 8     eeee eeeee eeeee ee   e eeeee eeeee
8   8 8    8 8eeee 8  8 8   8 8   8 88   8 8   8 8   "
8eee8 8eeee8     8 8e   8eee8 8e  8 88  e8 8eee8 8eeee
88      88       8 88   88  8 88  8  8  8  88  8    88
88      88   eeee8 88e8 88  8 88  8  8ee8  88  8 8ee88

```
A simple p5-inspired interface to the Canvas object
© Daniel Berio (@colormotor) 2023 - ...

NOTE: this is highly unoptimized, since it uses pycairo for rendering and copies all the screen at each frame
It will probably be significantly slow when using a large canvas size
